title: World Food Programme Guides Operations in Afghanistan with Online GIS Apps

indicators_measured.name: Prevalence of moderate or severe food insecurity in the population
Number of people affected by disasters
Score of adoption and implementation of national disaster-risk reduction strategies in line with the Sendai Framework
Number of refugees per 100,000 population, by country of origin

indicators_targeted.name: Prevalence of undernourishment
indicators_targeted.rationale: WFP aims to achieve zero hunger by targeting food-insecure households in remote and conflict-affected districts

indicators_targeted.name: Prevalence of moderate or severe food insecurity
indicators_targeted.rationale: Vulnerability mapping identifies populations needing food assistance

indicators_targeted.name: Number of people affected by disasters
indicators_targeted.rationale: Climate-related disasters (drought, floods, landslides) drive food insecurity and constrain delivery

summary: The World Food Programme in Afghanistan faced severe operational constraints from mountainous terrain, limited roads, armed conflict, and climate shocks (drought, floods, COVID-19) that left families food insecure. WFP built an integrated online GIS platform combining vulnerability analysis, security layers, supply-chain routes, road conditions, and programmatic data to support evidence-based humanitarian logistics. Drivers report real-time access constraints; security staff use conflict hotspot maps for access negotiations with non-state armed groups, replacing armed escorts with community acceptance policies. Methods include geospatial vulnerability assessment, multi-layer operational dashboards, field GPS reporting, and shared mapping with partners such as UNICEF. The approach operationalizes food security intelligence to plan deliveries, mitigate risk, and reach the most vulnerable populations.
